spotifyTitle: Company Law | M10 L10.1: Types of Resolutions
title: Company Law | M10 L10.1: Types of Resolutions
description: Companies make formal decisions by passing resolutions, but not all resolutions are the same. This episode distinguishes ordinary, special, and written resolutions under the Companies Act 2014, the voting thresholds required for each, and when each type must be used.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M10 L10.2: General Meetings - AGMs and EGMs
title: Company Law | M10 L10.2: General Meetings - AGMs and EGMs
description: The AGM and EGM are the formal mechanisms through which members exercise control over a company. We cover the requirements for convening each type of meeting, notice periods, quorum, voting rights, and the consequences of procedural defects. A practical topic for FE-1 corporate governance questions.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M10 L10.3: Director Meetings and Board Governance
title: Company Law | M10 L10.3: Director Meetings and Board Governance
description: How do directors make decisions, and what happens when proper board procedure is not followed? This episode covers board meetings, quorum, minutes, written resolutions of directors, and the legal effect of decisions made improperly. Governance procedure is tested more often than candidates expect.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M11 L11.1: Examinership
title: Company Law | M11 L11.1: Examinership
description: Examinership is Ireland's rescue procedure for insolvent but viable companies. This episode explains the process from petition to confirmation of a scheme of arrangement, the role of the examiner, and the protection from creditors during the process. A major topic for FE-1 corporate insolvency questions.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M11 L11.2: Receivership
title: Company Law | M11 L11.2: Receivership
description: A receiver is appointed by a secured creditor to realise security and recover a debt. We cover the appointment, powers, and duties of a receiver, the distinction between fixed and floating charge receivers, and the receiver's liability to the company and third parties. Often tested alongside charges.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M11 L11.3: Liquidation and Winding Up
title: Company Law | M11 L11.3: Liquidation and Winding Up
description: Winding up brings a company's life to an end. This episode covers voluntary and compulsory liquidation, the grounds for a winding up order, the appointment and role of the liquidator, and the order of priority for distributing assets. A comprehensive topic and a reliable FE-1 question area.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M11 L11.4: Voidable Transactions in Corporate Insolvency
title: Company Law | M11 L11.4: Voidable Transactions in Corporate Insolvency
description: When a company is wound up, certain pre-insolvency transactions can be set aside. This episode covers fraudulent preferences, reckless trading, related party transactions, and the liquidator's power to unwind them under the Companies Act 2014. Critical for FE-1 questions involving creditor protection on insolvency.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M12 L12.1: The Corporate Enforcement Authority
title: Company Law | M12 L12.1: The Corporate Enforcement Authority
description: The Corporate Enforcement Authority (CEA) replaced the ODCE and is responsible for investigating and prosecuting breaches of company law in Ireland. This episode covers its establishment, functions, investigative powers, and interaction with the Director of Public Prosecutions.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M1 L1.1: What is Company Law? The Nature of a Company
title: Company Law | M1 L1.1: What is Company Law? The Nature of a Company
description: This episode opens Company Law by examining what a company actually is and why it exists as a distinct legal entity. We look at the foundational nature of incorporation under the Companies Act 2014 and the consequences of legal personality. Essential groundwork for every other Company Law topic on the FE-1.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M1 L1.2: Advantages and Disadvantages of Incorporation
title: Company Law | M1 L1.2: Advantages and Disadvantages of Incorporation
description: We weigh the practical and legal benefits of incorporation — limited liability, perpetual succession, transferable shares — against the regulatory burdens that come with it. Understanding this balance is a common starting point for FE-1 problem questions on company formation.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M1 L1.3: Section 31 and the Statutory Contract
title: Company Law | M1 L1.3: Section 31 and the Statutory Contract
description: Section 31 of the Companies Act 2014 creates a statutory contract between the company and its members. This episode explains what that means in practice, who can enforce it, and the limits of enforcement. A frequently tested concept in FE-1 exam questions on member rights.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M1 L1.4: LTDs versus DACs - Key Differences
title: Company Law | M1 L1.4: LTDs versus DACs - Key Differences
description: Not all private companies are the same. This episode draws out the key distinctions between a Limited Company (LTD) and a Designated Activity Company (DAC) under the Companies Act 2014. Knowing which company type applies to a given scenario is essential for exam accuracy.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M2 L2.1: The Salomon Principle
title: Company Law | M2 L2.1: The Salomon Principle
description: Salomon v Salomon [1897] is the cornerstone of company law and this episode gives it the full treatment it deserves. We cover the separate legal personality rule, its origins, and its significance for creditors, shareholders, and directors. Expect this case to appear directly or indirectly in almost every FE-1 Company Law question.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M2 L2.2: Disregarding or Lifting the Corporate Veil
title: Company Law | M2 L2.2: Disregarding or Lifting the Corporate Veil
description: Sometimes courts will look behind the corporate structure and hold individuals personally liable — this is lifting the veil. We examine the statutory and judicial grounds for doing so, including fraud, agency, and the relevant Irish and UK case law. A core exam topic with a consistent question pattern.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M3 L3.1: Section 158 - Delegation of Management Powers
title: Company Law | M3 L3.1: Section 158 - Delegation of Management Powers
description: Section 158 of the Companies Act 2014 vests management of the company in the directors. This episode examines how that delegation works, what powers are reserved to members, and the constitutional balance within a company. Critical for questions on director authority and internal decision-making.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M3 L3.2: Corporate Capacity and the Indoor Management Rule
title: Company Law | M3 L3.2: Corporate Capacity and the Indoor Management Rule
description: Can a company be bound by a contract entered into without proper authority? This episode covers the Indoor Management Rule (the rule in Royal British Bank v Turquand), its statutory expression in the Companies Act 2014, and the protection it affords to third parties dealing with companies in good faith.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M4 L4.1: Fixed and Floating Charges - Definitions and Priority
title: Company Law | M4 L4.1: Fixed and Floating Charges - Definitions and Priority
description: Companies raise finance by granting charges over their assets. This episode explains the distinction between fixed and floating charges, the registration requirements under the Companies Act 2014, and how priority between competing charges is determined. Heavily tested in FE-1 insolvency and corporate finance questions.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M4 L4.2: Fixed vs Floating Charges and Crystallisation
title: Company Law | M4 L4.2: Fixed vs Floating Charges and Crystallisation
description: Building on the last episode, we dig into when a floating charge crystallises and becomes fixed, the consequences for the company and creditors, and the key Irish case law on characterising a charge. Exam questions often test whether candidates can correctly categorise a charge in a given set of facts.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M4 L4.3: Retention of Title Clauses
title: Company Law | M4 L4.3: Retention of Title Clauses
description: A retention of title clause allows a seller to retain ownership of goods until paid in full. We examine how these clauses operate under Irish law, the conditions for their validity, and how they interact with floating charges and insolvency proceedings. A practical topic that appears regularly in FE-1 commercial law scenarios.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M5 L5.1: Types of Directors and the Duty to Act in Good Faith
title: Company Law | M5 L5.1: Types of Directors and the Duty to Act in Good Faith
description: Not all directors are equal in law. This episode distinguishes executive, non-executive, shadow, and de facto directors, and explains the core fiduciary duty to act in good faith in the interests of the company under Section 228(1)(a) of the Companies Act 2014. The starting point for all director duty questions.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M5 L5.2: Conflict of Interest and the Duty of Skill, Care and Diligence
title: Company Law | M5 L5.2: Conflict of Interest and the Duty of Skill, Care and Diligence
description: Directors must avoid conflicts between their personal interests and their duties to the company, and must act with the care and skill of a reasonably diligent person. This episode works through both duties under the Companies Act 2014 with supporting Irish case law and practical exam application.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M5 L5.3: Remedies for Breach of Directors' Duties
title: Company Law | M5 L5.3: Remedies for Breach of Directors' Duties
description: What happens when a director breaches their fiduciary duties? We cover the full range of remedies available to the company — account of profits, injunction, rescission, and personal liability — and how courts have applied them in Irish and UK cases. Essential for any FE-1 question asking you to advise on director misconduct.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M6 L6.1: Section 238 and Substantial Non-Cash Asset Transactions
title: Company Law | M6 L6.1: Section 238 and Substantial Non-Cash Asset Transactions
description: Section 238 of the Companies Act 2014 restricts directors from acquiring or disposing of substantial non-cash assets with the company without member approval. This episode explains the thresholds, the approval process, and the consequences of non-compliance. A technical but regularly tested statutory provision.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M6 L6.2: Section 239 and Loans to Directors
title: Company Law | M6 L6.2: Section 239 and Loans to Directors
description: Section 239 restricts companies from making loans to directors without proper authorisation. We cover the scope of the prohibition, the exceptions, and the civil and criminal consequences of breach. Often paired with conflict of interest questions on the FE-1.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M7 L7.1: Section 212 - Oppression and Disregard of Members
title: Company Law | M7 L7.1: Section 212 - Oppression and Disregard of Members
description: Section 212 of the Companies Act 2014 protects minority shareholders from oppressive conduct by the majority or the company itself. This episode examines the statutory remedy, the case law defining oppression, and how Irish courts have applied the provision. A high-value topic for FE-1 minority shareholder questions.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M7 L7.2: Foss v Harbottle and the Derivative Action
title: Company Law | M7 L7.2: Foss v Harbottle and the Derivative Action
description: The rule in Foss v Harbottle (1843) prevents individual shareholders from suing on behalf of the company — but there are exceptions. This episode covers the rule, the fraud on the minority exception, and the statutory derivative action under the Companies Act 2014. A complex but examinable topic.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M8 L8.1: Restriction of Directors - Section 819
title: Company Law | M8 L8.1: Restriction of Directors - Section 819
description: Section 819 of the Companies Act 2014 provides for the restriction of directors of insolvent companies from acting as directors for five years. We examine the grounds, the process, the role of the Corporate Enforcement Authority, and the defences available. Appears frequently in FE-1 insolvency scenarios.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M8 L8.2: Disqualification of Directors - Section 839
title: Company Law | M8 L8.2: Disqualification of Directors - Section 839
description: Disqualification goes further than restriction and can result in a complete ban from acting as a company director. This episode covers the grounds for disqualification under Section 839, both automatic and court-ordered, and the consequences of acting while disqualified.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M9 L9.1: Dividends and Distributions
title: Company Law | M9 L9.1: Dividends and Distributions
description: A company can only pay dividends out of distributable profits — but what counts as distributable? This episode explains the rules on distributions under the Companies Act 2014, the consequences of unlawful dividends, and director liability for authorising improper payments. A deceptively technical topic.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Company Law | M9 L9.2: Transfer of Shares
title: Company Law | M9 L9.2: Transfer of Shares
description: Shares in a private company are not freely transferable — the articles typically restrict transfer. This episode covers the mechanics of share transfer, pre-emption rights, the role of the board in refusing a transfer, and the legal consequences of improper refusal.
subject: Company Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M1 L1: History and Origins of Bunreacht na hEireann
title: Constitutional Law | M1 L1: History and Origins of Bunreacht na hEireann
description: Ireland's 1937 Constitution did not emerge in a vacuum — it was shaped by the Free State Constitution of 1922, the political context of the time, and the influence of Catholic social teaching. This episode traces the historical and ideological origins of Bunreacht na hEireann and explains why that history still matters for constitutional interpretation today.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M1 L2: Constitutional Interpretation
title: Constitutional Law | M1 L2: Constitutional Interpretation
description: How do Irish courts read the Constitution? This episode examines the key approaches — literal, purposive, historical, and natural law — and the case law that illustrates when each applies. Understanding interpretive methodology is essential for structuring any FE-1 constitutional law answer.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M1 L3: Amendment of the Constitution - Articles 46 and 47
title: Constitutional Law | M1 L3: Amendment of the Constitution - Articles 46 and 47
description: The Constitution can only be amended by referendum under Articles 46 and 47. We examine the procedure for proposing and approving amendments, the role of the Oireachtas and the people, and the significance of key constitutional referendums in Ireland's legal history.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M2 L1: The Doctrine of Separation of Powers
title: Constitutional Law | M2 L1: The Doctrine of Separation of Powers
description: The separation of powers divides governmental authority between the legislature, executive, and judiciary. This episode examines the constitutional basis for that division in Ireland, the leading cases on encroachment between branches, and why the doctrine matters for the validity of legislation.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M2 L2: The Oireachtas and Legislative Power - Article 15
title: Constitutional Law | M2 L2: The Oireachtas and Legislative Power - Article 15
description: Article 15 vests the exclusive power to make laws in the Oireachtas. We examine what that exclusivity means, the consequences of sub-delegation, and the cases in which legislation has been struck down for impermissibly delegating legislative power. A key topic for separation of powers questions.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M2 L3: The Government and Executive Power - Article 28
title: Constitutional Law | M2 L3: The Government and Executive Power - Article 28
description: Article 28 vests executive power in the Government. This episode covers the collective responsibility of Cabinet, the constitutional conventions governing government formation, and the cases that have tested the boundaries of executive power in Ireland.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M2 L4: The Courts and Judicial Power - Articles 34 to 37
title: Constitutional Law | M2 L4: The Courts and Judicial Power - Articles 34 to 37
description: Articles 34 to 37 establish the court system and vest judicial power exclusively in the courts. We examine what counts as the administration of justice, the constitutional prohibition on non-judicial bodies exercising judicial power, and the landmark cases applying this principle — including McDonald v Bord na gCon.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M2 L5: The President - Articles 12 to 14
title: Constitutional Law | M2 L5: The President - Articles 12 to 14
description: The President occupies a unique constitutional position — head of state, but largely ceremonial. This episode covers the powers and functions of the President under Articles 12 to 14, including the absolute discretions and the limited but significant power to refer Bills to the Supreme Court under Article 26.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M2 L6: Delegated Legislation and Henry VIII Clauses
title: Constitutional Law | M2 L6: Delegated Legislation and Henry VIII Clauses
description: When the Oireachtas empowers a minister to make statutory instruments, constitutional limits apply. This episode covers the principles governing delegated legislation in Ireland, the controversial Henry VIII clause, and the cases that have struck down overbroad grants of legislative power.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M3 L1: Grounds and Procedure for Judicial Review
title: Constitutional Law | M3 L1: Grounds and Procedure for Judicial Review
description: Judicial review is the mechanism by which courts supervise the exercise of public power. This episode covers the procedural requirements under Order 84 RSC, the distinction between certiorari, mandamus, and prohibition, and the grounds on which public decisions can be challenged in Ireland.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M3 L2: Constitutional Remedies
title: Constitutional Law | M3 L2: Constitutional Remedies
description: When a constitutional right is breached, what remedies are available? This episode examines the remedies courts can grant for constitutional violations — declarations, injunctions, damages, and the writ of habeas corpus — and the principles that govern how courts exercise their remedial discretion.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M3 L3: The Article 26 Reference Procedure
title: Constitutional Law | M3 L3: The Article 26 Reference Procedure
description: Article 26 allows the President to refer a Bill to the Supreme Court to test its constitutionality before it is signed into law. This episode covers how the procedure works, its consequences (including the immunity of a bill that passes from future challenge), and the key Article 26 references in Irish constitutional history.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M4 L1: Equality Before the Law - Article 40.1
title: Constitutional Law | M4 L1: Equality Before the Law - Article 40.1
description: Article 40.1 guarantees that all citizens shall be held equal before the law. This episode examines what that equality guarantee means in practice, how Irish courts distinguish between permissible classification and unconstitutional discrimination, and the leading cases shaping this right.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M4 L2: Unenumerated Personal Rights - Article 40.3
title: Constitutional Law | M4 L2: Unenumerated Personal Rights - Article 40.3
description: Article 40.3 protects the personal rights of citizens — including rights not explicitly named in the Constitution. This episode traces the development of unenumerated rights doctrine from Ryan v AG [1965] through to the modern case law, and examines which rights have been recognised and which have been rejected.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M4 L3: Liberty of the Person - Article 40.4
title: Constitutional Law | M4 L3: Liberty of the Person - Article 40.4
description: Article 40.4 protects the right to personal liberty and provides for habeas corpus. We examine the constitutional guarantee against unlawful detention, the procedure for challenging detention, and the Irish cases that have shaped this fundamental right. A practical and exam-relevant topic.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M4 L4: Inviolability of the Dwelling - Article 40.5
title: Constitutional Law | M4 L4: Inviolability of the Dwelling - Article 40.5
description: Article 40.5 guarantees that the dwelling of every citizen is inviolable. This episode examines the scope of this protection, how it interacts with search warrant powers, and the cases in which courts have found breaches of this constitutional guarantee. Relevant for both constitutional and criminal law questions.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M4 L5: Freedom of Expression - Article 40.6.1(i)
title: Constitutional Law | M4 L5: Freedom of Expression - Article 40.6.1(i)
description: Freedom of expression is protected by Article 40.6.1(i) but it is not absolute — the Constitution itself permits restrictions for public order, morality, and the authority of the State. This episode maps out the constitutional framework for expression, the balancing approach of Irish courts, and the interaction with defamation and privacy law.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M4 L6: Freedom of Assembly and Association - Article 40.6.1(ii) and (iii)
title: Constitutional Law | M4 L6: Freedom of Assembly and Association - Article 40.6.1(ii) and (iii)
description: Articles 40.6.1(ii) and (iii) protect the rights to peaceful assembly and to form associations. We examine the scope of these rights, the constitutional grounds for restricting them, and the Irish cases that have tested the boundaries between lawful protest and criminal conduct.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M5 L1: The Family - Article 41
title: Constitutional Law | M5 L1: The Family - Article 41
description: Article 41 recognises the Family as the primary unit of society and provides it with constitutional protection. This episode examines the traditional and evolving definition of family under Irish constitutional law, the key cases on marital privacy, and the tension between Article 41 and the rights of individual family members.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M5 L2: Education - Article 42
title: Constitutional Law | M5 L2: Education - Article 42
description: Article 42 places primary responsibility for a child's education on parents, not the State. This episode covers the constitutional right to education, the limits of state intervention, the obligation to provide for free primary education, and the leading cases on the parent-state relationship in education.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M5 L3: Children's Rights - Article 42A
title: Constitutional Law | M5 L3: Children's Rights - Article 42A
description: Article 42A, inserted by referendum in 2012, gave express constitutional recognition to children's rights. This episode examines what Article 42A adds to the existing constitutional framework, the best interests of the child standard, and how the provision interacts with parental rights under Article 41.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M6 L1: Right to Private Property - Article 43
title: Constitutional Law | M6 L1: Right to Private Property - Article 43
description: Article 43 guarantees the right to private property but expressly permits the State to regulate its exercise in the interests of the common good. This episode covers the scope of the property right, the test for proportionate regulation versus unconstitutional interference, and the key Irish property rights cases.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M6 L2: Freedom of Religion - Article 44
title: Constitutional Law | M6 L2: Freedom of Religion - Article 44
description: Article 44 guarantees freedom of conscience and the practice of religion. We examine the separation of Church and State under the Irish Constitution, the prohibition on endowment of religion, and how courts have approached cases involving conflicts between religious freedom and other constitutional rights.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M6 L3: Directive Principles of Social Policy - Article 45
title: Constitutional Law | M6 L3: Directive Principles of Social Policy - Article 45
description: Article 45 sets out principles of social policy to guide the Oireachtas — but they are expressly stated to be non-justiciable. This episode examines what Article 45 contains, why it cannot be enforced in court, and how it nonetheless influences constitutional interpretation and legislative policy.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M7 L1: Due Course of Law - The Core Guarantee
title: Constitutional Law | M7 L1: Due Course of Law - The Core Guarantee
description: Article 38.1 provides that no person shall be tried on any criminal charge save in due course of law. This episode examines what that guarantee means, the constitutional requirements for a fair criminal trial, and how the due course of law standard sits alongside the specific rights in Articles 38 and 40.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M7 L2: Vague Offences
title: Constitutional Law | M7 L2: Vague Offences
description: The Constitution prohibits criminal liability being imposed on vague or uncertain offences. This episode examines the vagueness doctrine in Irish constitutional law, the test applied by courts, and the cases in which legislation has been struck down for failing to define a criminal offence with sufficient precision.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M7 L3: Presumption of Innocence and Reverse Onus
title: Constitutional Law | M7 L3: Presumption of Innocence and Reverse Onus
description: Every accused person is presumed innocent until proven guilty. This episode examines the constitutional status of the presumption of innocence, when the Oireachtas can legitimately reverse the burden of proof, and the test Irish courts apply to assess whether a reverse onus clause is constitutionally permissible.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M7 L4: Undue Delay
title: Constitutional Law | M7 L4: Undue Delay
description: A person accused of a criminal offence has a constitutional right to a trial with reasonable expedition. This episode covers the law on undue delay in Irish criminal proceedings — when delay becomes unconstitutional, the test from PM v Malone, and the remedy of prohibiting a trial on grounds of delay.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M7 L5: Duty to Seek and Preserve Evidence
title: Constitutional Law | M7 L5: Duty to Seek and Preserve Evidence
description: The State has a duty to seek out and preserve evidence that may be relevant to an accused's defence. This episode examines that constitutional obligation, the consequences of failure — including the possibility of a stay on proceedings — and the leading Irish cases on prosecutorial disclosure obligations.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M7 L6: Pre-Trial Publicity and the Fair Trial Guarantee
title: Constitutional Law | M7 L6: Pre-Trial Publicity and the Fair Trial Guarantee
description: Extensive media coverage of a criminal case can compromise a defendant's right to a fair trial. We examine when pre-trial publicity crosses a constitutional line, the test for granting a prohibition or change of venue, and how Irish courts balance freedom of expression with the right to a fair trial.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M7 L7: Fair Procedures and Natural Justice
title: Constitutional Law | M7 L7: Fair Procedures and Natural Justice
description: The constitutional right to fair procedures — audi alteram partem and nemo iudex in causa sua — applies wherever a decision affects a person's rights. This episode covers the natural justice principles, their constitutional basis, and the case law on what fair procedures actually require in practice.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M7 L8: The Special Criminal Court - Article 38.3
title: Constitutional Law | M7 L8: The Special Criminal Court - Article 38.3
description: Article 38.3 permits the establishment of special courts for the trial of offences where the ordinary courts are inadequate to secure the effective administration of justice. This episode examines the constitutional basis for the Special Criminal Court, the jury trial guarantee, and the case law on when referral to the SCC is constitutionally justified.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M7 L9: Right to Legal Representation and Legal Aid
title: Constitutional Law | M7 L9: Right to Legal Representation and Legal Aid
description: The right of access to a lawyer is a fundamental aspect of the right to a fair trial. This episode covers the constitutional right to legal representation in criminal proceedings, the State's obligation to provide legal aid where a person cannot afford a lawyer, and the key cases from Ireland and the ECtHR on this right.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M8 L1: International Law and Article 29 - Ireland's Dualist System
title: Constitutional Law | M8 L1: International Law and Article 29 - Ireland's Dualist System
description: Ireland operates a dualist system: international law does not automatically become part of Irish domestic law. Article 29 governs Ireland's relationship with the international legal order. This episode explains what that means, how treaties must be incorporated, and the consequences for Irish courts when interpreting rights with an international dimension.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M8 L2: Ireland and the EU - Article 29.4 and the Crotty Principle
title: Constitutional Law | M8 L2: Ireland and the EU - Article 29.4 and the Crotty Principle
description: Ireland's membership of the EU is constitutionally grounded in Article 29.4. This episode examines how EU law is accommodated in the Irish constitutional order, the Crotty principle requiring referendum approval for significant EU treaty changes, and the tension between EU primacy and Irish constitutional sovereignty.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M8 L3: Primacy of EU Law - The Irish Constitutional Position
title: Constitutional Law | M8 L3: Primacy of EU Law - The Irish Constitutional Position
description: EU law claims primacy over national law — but how does that sit with the Irish Constitution? This episode examines the Irish approach to the supremacy question, the cases that have addressed direct conflicts between EU law and constitutional provisions, and what happens when the two systems cannot be reconciled.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Constitutional Law | M8 L4: The ECHR in Irish Law
title: Constitutional Law | M8 L4: The ECHR in Irish Law
description: The European Convention on Human Rights was incorporated into Irish law by the ECHR Act 2003. This episode explains how the Convention operates in Irish domestic law, the interpretive obligation on courts, the declaration of incompatibility remedy, and the distinction between Convention rights and constitutional rights.
subject: Constitutional Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M1 L1.1: Offer and Acceptance
title: Contract Law | M1 L1.1: Offer and Acceptance
description: The formation of a contract begins with offer and acceptance, and getting this right is the foundation of the entire subject. This episode works through the rules on offer, invitation to treat, acceptance, communication, and revocation — with the key Irish and English case law that FE-1 candidates must know.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M1 L1.2: Consideration
title: Contract Law | M1 L1.2: Consideration
description: No contract is binding without consideration — or is it? This episode examines the rules on consideration, the requirement that it be sufficient but not adequate, part payment of debt, past consideration, and the performance of existing duties. A technically demanding topic that rewards clear thinking.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M1 L1.3: Promissory Estoppel
title: Contract Law | M1 L1.3: Promissory Estoppel
description: Promissory estoppel allows a party to enforce a promise even without consideration in certain circumstances. We examine the doctrine from its origins in Central London Property Trust v High Trees, its development in Irish law, and the critical question of whether estoppel can be used as a sword or only a shield.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M1 L1.4: Intention to Create Legal Relations
title: Contract Law | M1 L1.4: Intention to Create Legal Relations
description: Not every agreement is a contract — the parties must intend to be legally bound. This episode covers the presumptions that apply in domestic and commercial contexts, how those presumptions can be rebutted, and the Irish cases where courts have had to decide whether parties truly intended legal relations.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M1 L1.5: Capacity to Contract
title: Contract Law | M1 L1.5: Capacity to Contract
description: Who can legally enter into a binding contract? This episode covers the capacity rules for minors, persons of unsound mind, intoxicated persons, and companies, including the special rules that apply to contracts made by or with minors under Irish law.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M1 L1.6: Formal Requirements
title: Contract Law | M1 L1.6: Formal Requirements
description: Most contracts can be made orally, but some must be in writing or evidenced in writing to be enforceable. This episode covers the Statute of Frauds, contracts that require written form under Irish law, and the consequences of non-compliance including part performance as an equitable exception.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M2 L2.1: Express Terms and Representations
title: Contract Law | M2 L2.1: Express Terms and Representations
description: What is actually in the contract? This episode distinguishes statements that are contractual terms from those that are mere representations, explains how courts determine what has been incorporated, and covers the parol evidence rule and its exceptions. A fundamental distinction for FE-1 problem questions.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M2 L2.2: Conditions, Warranties and Innominate Terms
title: Contract Law | M2 L2.2: Conditions, Warranties and Innominate Terms
description: Not all contractual terms carry the same consequences for breach. This episode distinguishes conditions (breach entitles termination) from warranties (breach sounds only in damages) and innominate terms, and works through the Irish and English cases on how courts classify terms and choose the appropriate remedy.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M2 L2.3: Implied Terms and Interpretation
title: Contract Law | M2 L2.3: Implied Terms and Interpretation
description: Terms can be implied into contracts by statute, by custom, or by the courts. This episode covers the tests for implying terms in fact and in law, the business efficacy and officious bystander tests, the modern approach to contractual interpretation, and the Consumer Rights Act 2022 implied terms.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M2 L2.4: Exemption Clauses
title: Contract Law | M2 L2.4: Exemption Clauses
description: A party may try to exclude or limit liability through an exemption clause — but courts apply strict rules to them. We cover incorporation by signature, notice, and course of dealing; the contra proferentem rule; and the statutory controls under the Consumer Rights Act 2022 and Sale of Goods legislation.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M2 L2.5: Certainty of Terms
title: Contract Law | M2 L2.5: Certainty of Terms
description: A contract must be sufficiently certain to be enforceable. This episode examines when vagueness or incompleteness will prevent a contract from being binding, the mechanisms courts use to save uncertain agreements, and the distinction between agreements to agree and enforceable contracts.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M3 L3.1: Consumer Rights Act 2022 - Sale of Goods
title: Contract Law | M3 L3.1: Consumer Rights Act 2022 - Sale of Goods
description: The Consumer Rights Act 2022 fundamentally overhauled consumer protection law in Ireland. This episode covers the implied terms as to title, description, quality, and fitness for purpose in contracts for the sale of goods, and the remedies available to consumers when goods do not conform.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M3 L3.2: Consumer Rights Act 2022 - Digital Content and Services
title: Contract Law | M3 L3.2: Consumer Rights Act 2022 - Digital Content and Services
description: The 2022 Act broke new ground by extending statutory protections to contracts for digital content and digital services. This episode covers the conformity requirements for digital products, the remedies available, and why this is an increasingly important area for FE-1 candidates to understand.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M3 L3.3: Unfair Contract Terms
title: Contract Law | M3 L3.3: Unfair Contract Terms
description: Consumer contracts must not contain unfair terms. This episode examines the unfairness test under the Consumer Rights Act 2022, the indicative list of potentially unfair terms, the consequences of a term being found unfair, and how these protections sit alongside the common law rules on exemption clauses.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M4 L4.1: Misrepresentation
title: Contract Law | M4 L4.1: Misrepresentation
description: A misrepresentation is a false statement of fact made before the contract is concluded that induces the other party to enter. This episode covers the types of misrepresentation — fraudulent, negligent, and innocent — the available remedies including rescission and damages, and the Irish cases that illustrate the distinctions.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M4 L4.2: Mistake
title: Contract Law | M4 L4.2: Mistake
description: Mistake is one of the most complex vitiating factors in contract law. This episode examines common mistake, mutual mistake, and unilateral mistake, when mistake will void or voidable a contract at common law and in equity, and the leading cases from Ireland and England.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M4 L4.3: Duress
title: Contract Law | M4 L4.3: Duress
description: A contract obtained through unlawful pressure — duress — may be voidable. This episode covers physical duress, economic duress, the development of the doctrine in Irish and English case law, and the distinction between legitimate commercial pressure and illegitimate coercion.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M4 L4.4: Undue Influence
title: Contract Law | M4 L4.4: Undue Influence
description: Where one party exploits a position of influence over another to procure a contract, the doctrine of undue influence may apply. We cover actual and presumed undue influence, the relationships that give rise to a presumption, the role of independent legal advice, and the landmark cases on third-party undue influence in banking transactions.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M5 L5.1: Discharge by Agreement and Performance
title: Contract Law | M5 L5.1: Discharge by Agreement and Performance
description: A contract can come to an end in a number of ways. This episode covers discharge by performance — including the rules on exact and substantial performance — and discharge by agreement, examining when parties can validly agree to bring their obligations to an end.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M5 L5.2: Discharge by Breach and Repudiation
title: Contract Law | M5 L5.2: Discharge by Breach and Repudiation
description: A sufficiently serious breach by one party can entitle the innocent party to terminate the contract. This episode covers the right to terminate for breach, anticipatory breach and repudiation, the election to affirm or accept repudiation, and the consequences of wrongful termination.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M5 L5.3: Discharge by Frustration
title: Contract Law | M5 L5.3: Discharge by Frustration
description: The doctrine of frustration applies when a supervening event makes performance of a contract radically different from what was agreed. We examine the test for frustration from Davis Contractors v Fareham, the types of events that can frustrate a contract, and the Irish case law — including the consequences under the Civil Liability (Amendment) Act 1961.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M6 L6.1: Damages
title: Contract Law | M6 L6.1: Damages
description: Damages are the primary remedy for breach of contract. This episode covers the expectation, reliance, and restitution measures of loss, the rules on remoteness from Hadley v Baxendale, the duty to mitigate, and the assessment of damages in Irish contract cases. A topic that rewards methodical exam technique.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M6 L6.2: Equitable Remedies in Contract
title: Contract Law | M6 L6.2: Equitable Remedies in Contract
description: Specific performance and injunction are the main equitable remedies for breach of contract. This episode examines when specific performance will be granted, the bars to the remedy, and how injunctions are used to enforce negative contractual obligations — with reference to Irish case law throughout.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M7 L7.1: Privity of Contract
title: Contract Law | M7 L7.1: Privity of Contract
description: The doctrine of privity provides that only parties to a contract can enforce it or be bound by it. This episode examines the doctrine, the hardship it can cause, the common law and equitable exceptions, and the statutory modifications introduced in Ireland by the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Contract Law | M7 L7.2: Illegal and Void Contracts
title: Contract Law | M7 L7.2: Illegal and Void Contracts
description: Some contracts are unenforceable because they are contrary to law or public policy. This episode covers contracts illegal at formation and contracts that become illegal in performance, contracts void for illegality, contracts in restraint of trade, and how courts apportion consequences between the parties.
subject: Contract Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M10 L10.1: Duress and Self-Defence
title: Criminal Law | M10 L10.1: Duress and Self-Defence
description: Where a person commits a crime under a threat of death or serious injury, the defence of duress may apply. This episode covers the elements of duress, its limitations (particularly in murder cases), and the law on self-defence under Section 18 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M10 L10.2: Defence of the Dwelling
title: Criminal Law | M10 L10.2: Defence of the Dwelling
description: Section 2 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 and the Criminal Law (Defence and the Dwelling) Act 2011 provide a specific statutory framework for using force to defend one's home. This episode examines the scope of the defence, the force that is permissible, and how Irish courts have interpreted the dwelling protection.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M10 L10.2: Defence of the Dwelling
title: Criminal Law | M10 L10.2: Defence of the Dwelling
description: Section 2 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 and the Criminal Law (Defence and the Dwelling) Act 2011 provide a specific statutory framework for using force to defend one's home. This episode examines the scope of the defence, the force that is permissible, and how Irish courts have interpreted the dwelling protection.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M11 L11.1: DVA 2018, Coercive Control and the Coco Act
title: Criminal Law | M11 L11.1: DVA 2018, Coercive Control and the Coco Act
description: The Domestic Violence Act 2018 and the Criminal Justice (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2023 (the Coco Act) represent a major development in Irish criminal law. This episode covers the offence of coercive control, the civil orders available under the DVA 2018, and the interaction between civil and criminal remedies for domestic abuse.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M12 L12.1: Public Order Offences
title: Criminal Law | M12 L12.1: Public Order Offences
description: The Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994 governs public order in Ireland. This episode covers the main offences — threatening, abusive, or insulting behaviour, riot, violent disorder, and affray — their elements, and how Irish courts have balanced public order enforcement with constitutional rights to assembly and expression.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M12 L12.2: Offences Against Justice and State Offences
title: Criminal Law | M12 L12.2: Offences Against Justice and State Offences
description: This episode covers a range of offences directed at the administration of justice and the security of the State — including perjury, perverting the course of justice, contempt of court, and offences under the Offences Against the State Act 1939. Less glamorous but important for a complete FE-1 knowledge base.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M13 L13.1: Courts, Classification and Arrest
title: Criminal Law | M13 L13.1: Courts, Classification and Arrest
description: The structure of the Irish criminal court system determines where a case is tried and what procedural rules apply. This episode covers the courts' jurisdiction, the classification of offences for trial purposes, the law on arrest and detention under the Criminal Justice Act 1984, and the constitutional limits on Garda powers.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M13 L13.2: Bail and the Right to Silence
title: Criminal Law | M13 L13.2: Bail and the Right to Silence
description: This episode covers two constitutional rights with significant practical implications. We examine the right to bail, the grounds for refusal under the Bail Act 1997 and Article 40.4, and the controversial erosion of the right to silence — particularly the adverse inference provisions introduced by the Criminal Justice Act 2007.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M1 L1.1: Characteristics and Indicia of a Crime
title: Criminal Law | M1 L1.1: Characteristics and Indicia of a Crime
description: What makes an act a crime rather than a civil wrong? This episode examines the essential characteristics that distinguish criminal from civil liability, the role of the State in prosecution, and the general indicia courts use to classify an obligation as criminal in nature. The conceptual foundation for the entire subject.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M1 L1.2: Classification of Criminal Offences
title: Criminal Law | M1 L1.2: Classification of Criminal Offences
description: Irish criminal offences are classified in different ways — indictable vs summary, arrestable vs non-arrestable. This episode covers the classification system, why it matters for procedure, trial venue, and sentencing, and the impact of the Criminal Law Act 1997 on arrest powers.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M2 L2.1: Actus Reus - Core Principles, Voluntariness and Omissions
title: Criminal Law | M2 L2.1: Actus Reus - Core Principles, Voluntariness and Omissions
description: Every crime requires an act (actus reus) — but what counts? This episode examines the voluntariness requirement, the general rule that omissions cannot ground criminal liability, and the narrow exceptions where a duty to act has been recognised. Precise knowledge of the actus reus is essential for problem questions.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M2 L2.2: Causation
title: Criminal Law | M2 L2.2: Causation
description: Where a crime requires a result, the prosecution must prove the accused caused it. This episode covers factual causation (the but-for test), legal causation, the year-and-a-day rule (now abolished), novus actus interveniens, and the Irish case law on causation in homicide cases.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M3 L3.1: Mens Rea - Intention, Recklessness and Negligence
title: Criminal Law | M3 L3.1: Mens Rea - Intention, Recklessness and Negligence
description: Mens rea is the mental element of a crime. This episode covers the full range — specific intent, basic intent, subjective and objective recklessness, negligence, and strict liability — with the Irish and English cases that have shaped the modern law. Getting mens rea right is the difference between a pass and a fail in most FE-1 criminal scenarios.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M3 L3.1: Mens Rea - Intention, Recklessness and Negligence
title: Criminal Law | M3 L3.1: Mens Rea - Intention, Recklessness and Negligence
description: Mens rea is the mental element of a crime. This episode covers the full range — specific intent, basic intent, subjective and objective recklessness, negligence, and strict liability — with the Irish and English cases that have shaped the modern law. Getting mens rea right is the difference between a pass and a fail in most FE-1 criminal scenarios.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M4 L4.1: Murder - Definition, Mens Rea and Capital Murder
title: Criminal Law | M4 L4.1: Murder - Definition, Mens Rea and Capital Murder
description: Murder is the most serious offence in the criminal calendar. This episode covers the common law and statutory definition of murder in Ireland, the mens rea of malice aforethought, the distinction between murder and capital murder under the Criminal Justice Act 1964, and how intention is established in practice.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M4 L4.2: Voluntary Manslaughter and Provocation
title: Criminal Law | M4 L4.2: Voluntary Manslaughter and Provocation
description: A killing that would otherwise be murder may be reduced to manslaughter if the accused was provoked. This episode examines the defence of provocation in Irish law, the objective and subjective elements, the standard applied, and how the Law Reform Commission has proposed reforming this area.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M4 L4.3: Involuntary Manslaughter and Dangerous Driving Causing Death
title: Criminal Law | M4 L4.3: Involuntary Manslaughter and Dangerous Driving Causing Death
description: Involuntary manslaughter covers unlawful killings where the accused did not intend to kill. We examine unlawful act manslaughter and gross negligence manslaughter, the dangerous driving offence under the Road Traffic Act, and how Irish courts have approached causation and recklessness in fatal accident cases.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M4 L4.4: Assisted Suicide and the Right to Die
title: Criminal Law | M4 L4.4: Assisted Suicide and the Right to Die
description: This episode examines the law on assisted suicide and euthanasia in Ireland, the offence under Section 2 of the Criminal Law (Suicide) Act 1993, and the constitutional challenges that have been brought before the Irish courts and the European Court of Human Rights on the right to die with dignity.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M5 L5.1: Assault, Assault Causing Harm and Serious Harm
title: Criminal Law | M5 L5.1: Assault, Assault Causing Harm and Serious Harm
description: The Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 restructured the law on assault in Ireland. This episode covers Section 2 (assault), Section 3 (assault causing harm), and Section 4 (causing serious harm), the mens rea requirements for each, and the hierarchy of offences that FE-1 candidates need to navigate in problem questions.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M5 L5.2: Harassment and Stalking - NFOATP 1997
title: Criminal Law | M5 L5.2: Harassment and Stalking - NFOATP 1997
description: Section 10 and Section 10A of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 criminalise harassment and stalking respectively. This episode covers the elements of each offence, the conduct that can constitute harassment or stalking, and the role of the Harassment, Harmful Communications and Related Offences Act 2020.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M6 L6.1: Rape - Definition, Consent and the Abolition of Honest Belief
title: Criminal Law | M6 L6.1: Rape - Definition, Consent and the Abolition of Honest Belief
description: The law on rape in Ireland was significantly reformed by the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2017. This episode covers the definition of rape, the consent framework introduced by the 2017 Act, the abolition of the honest belief defence, and what these changes mean for prosecuting sexual violence in Ireland.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M6 L6.2: Sexual Assault and Rape Under Section 4
title: Criminal Law | M6 L6.2: Sexual Assault and Rape Under Section 4
description: Beyond rape, Irish law recognises several other serious sexual offences. This episode covers sexual assault under Section 2 of the Criminal Law (Rape) Amendment Act 1990, rape under Section 4, and the consent and mens rea requirements that apply across these offences.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M6 L6.3: Sexual Offences Against Children and Protected Persons
title: Criminal Law | M6 L6.3: Sexual Offences Against Children and Protected Persons
description: Irish law provides specific and enhanced protections for children and protected persons against sexual offences. This episode covers the relevant offences, the age of consent, strict liability provisions, and the obligations on professionals who become aware of such offences.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M7 L7.1: Theft and Deception
title: Criminal Law | M7 L7.1: Theft and Deception
description: The Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001 modernised Irish property crime law. This episode covers the definition of theft, the requirement of dishonesty, the deception offences, and the case law on what constitutes appropriation and intention to permanently deprive. Property offences are a staple of FE-1 criminal problem questions.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M7 L7.2: Burglary and Aggravated Burglary
title: Criminal Law | M7 L7.2: Burglary and Aggravated Burglary
description: Burglary involves entering a building as a trespasser with intent to commit certain offences. This episode examines the elements of burglary and aggravated burglary under the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001, the meaning of 'building', and how courts have approached the intent requirement.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M7 L7.3: Handling Stolen Goods, Criminal Damage and Cybercrime
title: Criminal Law | M7 L7.3: Handling Stolen Goods, Criminal Damage and Cybercrime
description: This episode covers three important property offences: handling stolen goods under the 2001 Act, criminal damage under the Criminal Damage Act 1991, and the emerging area of cybercrime under the Criminal Justice (Offences Relating to Information Systems) Act 2017.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M8 L8.1: Aid, Abet, Counsel and Procure - Secondary Liability
title: Criminal Law | M8 L8.1: Aid, Abet, Counsel and Procure - Secondary Liability
description: Criminal liability is not limited to the person who physically commits the offence. This episode covers the four modes of secondary participation — aiding, abetting, counselling, and procuring — under Section 7 of the Criminal Law Act 1997, the mens rea required for each, and the Irish case law on accessories.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M8 L8.2: Common Design, Joint Enterprise and Withdrawal
title: Criminal Law | M8 L8.2: Common Design, Joint Enterprise and Withdrawal
description: Where two or more people agree to commit a crime together, each may be liable for acts done in pursuance of that common design — including acts they did not personally commit. This episode covers common design liability, the extent to which it can extend to unforeseen acts, and the conditions under which a participant can withdraw.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Criminal Law | M9 L9.1: Conspiracy, Incitement and Attempt
title: Criminal Law | M9 L9.1: Conspiracy, Incitement and Attempt
description: Incomplete crimes can still attract criminal liability. This episode covers the inchoate offences of conspiracy, incitement, and attempt — the required acts and mental elements for each, the point at which liability begins, and the impossibility defence. These offences appear regularly in FE-1 problem scenarios.
subject: Criminal Law

spotifyTitle: Equity | M1 L1: Foundations of Equity - The Equitable Maxims
title: Equity | M1 L1: Foundations of Equity - The Equitable Maxims
description: Equity developed as a body of law administered by the Court of Chancery to mitigate the harshness of the common law. This episode introduces the historical development of equity, the fusion of common law and equity courts, and the maxims that underpin equitable doctrine — the principles that run through every topic in this subject.
subject: Equity

spotifyTitle: Equity | M2 L1: The Three Certainties - Certainty of Intention and Subject Matter
title: Equity | M2 L1: The Three Certainties - Certainty of Intention and Subject Matter
description: For an express trust to be valid, three certainties must be present. This episode examines the first two — certainty of intention (the requirement that the settlor clearly intends to create a trust) and certainty of subject matter (the requirement that the trust property be identifiable) — with the leading case law from Ireland and England.
subject: Equity

spotifyTitle: Equity | M2 L2: The Three Certainties - Certainty of Objects
title: Equity | M2 L2: The Three Certainties - Certainty of Objects
description: The third certainty requires that the beneficiaries of a trust be identifiable. This episode covers the different tests for certainty of objects that apply to fixed trusts and discretionary trusts, the consequences of failing the test, and the leading cases including McPhail v Doulton [1971] and Re Baden's Deed Trusts [1973].
subject: Equity

spotifyTitle: Equity | M4 L1: Secret Trusts - Fully Secret and Half-Secret Trusts
title: Equity | M4 L1: Secret Trusts - Fully Secret and Half-Secret Trusts
description: A secret trust allows a testator to communicate the true purpose of a bequest to the legatee without it appearing on the face of the will. This episode examines the distinction between fully secret and half-secret trusts, the requirements for their validity, and the theoretical basis for their enforcement outside the Wills Act.
subject: Equity

spotifyTitle: Equity | M4 L2: Mutual Wills
title: Equity | M4 L2: Mutual Wills
description: Mutual wills arise where two testators agree to make wills in agreed terms and not to revoke them. This episode covers the doctrine of mutual wills, when the agreement becomes binding and irrevocable, how a constructive trust is imposed on the surviving testator's estate, and the Irish and English cases on this difficult area.
subject: Equity

spotifyTitle: Equity | M5 L1: Trustees' Duties - Investment and the Prudent Trustee Standard
title: Equity | M5 L1: Trustees' Duties - Investment and the Prudent Trustee Standard
description: Trustees must invest trust property prudently. This episode covers the duty of investment under the Trustee Act 1893 and the Charities Act 2009, the prudent trustee standard, the requirement for diversification, and the cases that have defined what counts as imprudent investment leading to personal liability.
subject: Equity

spotifyTitle: Equity | M5 L2: Trustees' Duties - Duty Not to Profit and Self-Dealing
title: Equity | M5 L2: Trustees' Duties - Duty Not to Profit and Self-Dealing
description: A trustee must not profit from their position or place themselves in a position of conflict. This episode covers the no-profit rule, the self-dealing rule, the fair dealing rule, and the cases — including Keech v Sandford and Boardman v Phipps — that illustrate how strictly equity enforces these obligations.
subject: Equity

spotifyTitle: Equity | M5 L3: Trustees' Powers - Delegation, Advancement and Maintenance
title: Equity | M5 L3: Trustees' Powers - Delegation, Advancement and Maintenance
description: Trustees have both duties and powers. This episode covers the power to delegate, the conditions under which delegation is permitted and the trustee's liability for delegates, the statutory power of advancement under the Succession Act 1965, and the power of maintenance for infant beneficiaries.
subject: Equity

spotifyTitle: Equity | M6 L1: Resulting Trusts - Presumed and Automatic
title: Equity | M6 L1: Resulting Trusts - Presumed and Automatic
description: A resulting trust arises where property returns to the person who transferred it. This episode distinguishes between automatic resulting trusts (where a trust fails) and presumed resulting trusts (arising from voluntary transfer and purchase money contributions), the presumption of advancement, and how Irish courts have applied the resulting trust doctrine in family property disputes.
subject: Equity

spotifyTitle: Equity | M10 L1: Specific Performance and Other Equitable Remedies
title: Equity | M10 L1: Specific Performance and Other Equitable Remedies
description: Specific performance compels a party to perform their contractual obligations rather than simply pay damages. This episode covers when specific performance will be granted, the bars to the remedy, rectification, rescission, and account of profits — the full toolkit of equitable remedies that FE-1 candidates need to know.
subject: Equity

spotifyTitle: Equity | M3 L1: Constitution of Trusts - The Rule in Milroy v Lord
title: Equity | M3 L1: Constitution of Trusts - The Rule in Milroy v Lord
description: A trust must be properly constituted to be enforceable. The rule in Milroy v Lord [1862] states that equity will not perfect an imperfect gift. This episode examines what is required to constitute a trust, the different modes of transfer for different types of property, and the consequences of failure to constitute.
subject: Equity

spotifyTitle: Equity | M3 L2: Constitution of Trusts - Exceptions and Equity Will Perfect
title: Equity | M3 L2: Constitution of Trusts - Exceptions and Equity Will Perfect
description: The general rule that equity will not perfect an imperfect gift has important exceptions. This episode covers the rule in Strong v Bird, donatio mortis causa, proprietary estoppel as a constituting mechanism, and the modern cases that have stretched the boundaries of what equity will treat as constituted.
subject: Equity

spotifyTitle: Equity | M7 L1: Constructive Trusts - Categories and Common Intention
title: Equity | M7 L1: Constructive Trusts - Categories and Common Intention
description: A constructive trust is imposed by law regardless of the parties' intentions. This episode covers the main categories of constructive trust in Irish and English law, with particular focus on the common intention constructive trust as it applies to shared homes — a topic of enormous practical importance and regular FE-1 examination.
subject: Equity

spotifyTitle: Equity | M7 L2: Constructive Trusts and Proprietary Estoppel
title: Equity | M7 L2: Constructive Trusts and Proprietary Estoppel
description: Proprietary estoppel arises where a person relies on an assurance about property to their detriment. This episode examines the elements of proprietary estoppel, how it relates to and differs from the constructive trust, the remedies available, and the landmark Irish and English cases in this fast-developing area.
subject: Equity

spotifyTitle: Equity | M8 L2: Third-Party Liability - Knowing Receipt and Dishonest Assistance
title: Equity | M8 L2: Third-Party Liability - Knowing Receipt and Dishonest Assistance
description: Where a third party receives trust property or assists in a breach of trust, they may be personally liable in equity. This episode covers the two heads of third-party liability — knowing receipt and dishonest assistance — the mental element required for each, and the House of Lords decision in Royal Brunei Airlines v Tan that reshaped the law.
subject: Equity

spotifyTitle: Equity | M9 L1: Injunctions - Types and the Campus Oil Test
title: Equity | M9 L1: Injunctions - Types and the Campus Oil Test
description: The injunction is equity's most powerful remedy. This episode covers prohibitory and mandatory injunctions, the interlocutory injunction and the Campus Oil test as applied in Ireland, the Mareva injunction, and the Anton Piller order. A practical topic with clear exam application.
subject: Equity

spotifyTitle: Module Bonus Lesson 1
title: Module Bonus Lesson 1
subject: Equity

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M10 L1: Free Movement of Workers - Article 45 TFEU
title: EU Law | M10 L1: Free Movement of Workers - Article 45 TFEU
description: Article 45 TFEU guarantees free movement of workers within the EU. This episode covers the definition of 'worker', the rights conferred — including equal treatment, access to employment, social and tax advantages — and the Regulation 492/2011 framework that operationalises the right.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M10 L2: Workers - Family Members, Jobseekers and Rights After Employment
title: EU Law | M10 L2: Workers - Family Members, Jobseekers and Rights After Employment
description: The right to free movement extends beyond the worker themselves. This episode covers the rights of family members (including non-EU nationals) under Directive 2004/38, the rights of jobseekers, and what happens to EU law rights when a worker stops working — including rights of permanent residence.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M10 L3: Derogations from Free Movement of Workers
title: EU Law | M10 L3: Derogations from Free Movement of Workers
description: Free movement of workers is not absolute — Member States may restrict it on grounds of public policy, public security, and public health. This episode examines the derogations under Directive 2004/38, the requirement of proportionality, the procedural safeguards for those facing expulsion, and the case law from the CJEU.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M11 L1: EU Citizenship - Origins, Nature and the Maastricht Innovation
title: EU Law | M11 L1: EU Citizenship - Origins, Nature and the Maastricht Innovation
description: EU citizenship was introduced by the Maastricht Treaty and gives every national of an EU Member State a direct relationship with the European Union. This episode covers the origins of citizenship, its relationship to national citizenship, and the foundational case law — including Grzelczyk and Baumbast — that has defined its content.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M11 L2: Right of Residence Under Directive 2004/38 - Conditions and the Welfare Dimension
title: EU Law | M11 L2: Right of Residence Under Directive 2004/38 - Conditions and the Welfare Dimension
description: Directive 2004/38 consolidates and codifies EU citizenship and residence rights. This episode covers the three-tier structure of residence rights — up to three months, three months to five years, and permanent residence — the conditions that apply at each stage, and the Court's approach to welfare tourism claims.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M11 L3: Third Country National Family Members - Zambrano, McCarthy and Dereci
title: EU Law | M11 L3: Third Country National Family Members - Zambrano, McCarthy and Dereci
description: The rights of non-EU family members of EU citizens can be highly complex. This episode examines the cases — Zambrano, McCarthy, and Dereci — that have tested the boundaries of EU citizenship rights, the principle that EU law must not deprive a citizen of the genuine enjoyment of their citizenship, and what these cases mean for Irish immigration law.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M11 L4: Political Rights, Consular Protection and the Constitutional Significance of EU Citizenship
title: EU Law | M11 L4: Political Rights, Consular Protection and the Constitutional Significance of EU Citizenship
description: EU citizenship comes with political rights — including the right to vote and stand in European Parliament and municipal elections in any Member State. This episode also covers consular protection for EU citizens abroad, and reflects on the constitutional significance of a supranational citizenship that supplements but does not replace national citizenship.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M12 L1: The Equal Pay Principle - Article 157 TFEU and Defrenne
title: EU Law | M12 L1: The Equal Pay Principle - Article 157 TFEU and Defrenne
description: Article 157 TFEU requires equal pay for equal work between men and women. This episode examines the history of the equal pay principle, the landmark Defrenne v Sabena trilogy, the concept of 'pay' under EU law, and the relationship between Article 157 and the Equal Pay Directive.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M12 L2: Equal Treatment Directives and the Broadened Equality Framework
title: EU Law | M12 L2: Equal Treatment Directives and the Broadened Equality Framework
description: The EU's equality framework extends well beyond pay to cover access to employment, working conditions, and social security. This episode covers the Equal Treatment Directive, the Recast Directive 2006/54, and the expansion of EU equality law to grounds beyond sex — including race, religion, disability, and age.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M13 L1: Freedom of Establishment - Article 49 TFEU
title: EU Law | M13 L1: Freedom of Establishment - Article 49 TFEU
description: Article 49 TFEU prohibits restrictions on the freedom of EU nationals to establish themselves in business in another Member State. This episode covers the scope of the establishment right, the distinction between establishment and services, the leading cases, and the permitted grounds for restricting establishment.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M13 L2: Free Movement of Services - Article 56 TFEU and the Services Directive
title: EU Law | M13 L2: Free Movement of Services - Article 56 TFEU and the Services Directive
description: Article 56 TFEU prohibits restrictions on the freedom to provide services across borders. This episode examines the scope of the right, its relationship with establishment, the Services Directive 2006/123 and the country of origin principle, and the cases that have tested what restrictions on services are compatible with EU law.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M1 L1: Origins of European Integration - Post-War to the EEC
title: EU Law | M1 L1: Origins of European Integration - Post-War to the EEC
description: The European Union did not emerge fully formed. This episode traces the origins of European integration from the post-war political context through the establishment of the ECSC, EEC, and Euratom in the 1950s. Understanding why the EU was created is essential context for understanding how it operates and why it is constitutionally unique.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M1 L2: From the Single European Act to the Lisbon Treaty
title: EU Law | M1 L2: From the Single European Act to the Lisbon Treaty
description: The EU's constitutional framework has evolved through a series of treaty revisions. This episode covers the key developments from the Single European Act 1986 through Maastricht, Amsterdam, Nice, and the Lisbon Treaty 2007, focusing on the changes most relevant to FE-1 candidates — competence, institutions, and the Charter.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M1 L3: Brexit and Article 50 TEU
title: EU Law | M1 L3: Brexit and Article 50 TEU
description: The United Kingdom's withdrawal from the European Union was the first use of Article 50 TEU — the withdrawal mechanism. This episode examines the Article 50 procedure, the legal and constitutional issues it raised, the Withdrawal Agreement, and the ongoing relevance of Brexit for Irish law — including the Protocol/Windsor Framework.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M2 L1: The European Parliament - Composition, Powers and Democratic Role
title: EU Law | M2 L1: The European Parliament - Composition, Powers and Democratic Role
description: The European Parliament is the EU's directly elected institution. This episode covers its composition, legislative powers (co-decision/ordinary legislative procedure), budgetary role, scrutiny functions, and the ongoing debate about its role in addressing the EU's democratic deficit.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M2 L2: The Council and European Council - Intergovernmental Power
title: EU Law | M2 L2: The Council and European Council - Intergovernmental Power
description: The Council of the EU represents the governments of the Member States and is the EU's primary legislative chamber. This episode distinguishes the Council from the European Council, covers voting arrangements (unanimity, QMV), and examines the intergovernmental dimension of EU decision-making.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M2 L3: The European Commission - Guardian of the Treaties
title: EU Law | M2 L3: The European Commission - Guardian of the Treaties
description: The Commission is the engine of European integration — it proposes legislation, enforces EU law, and manages EU programmes. This episode covers the Commission's composition, appointment, legislative initiative monopoly, enforcement role under Article 258 TFEU, and its relationship with the other institutions.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M2 L4: The Court of Justice - Structure, Jurisdiction and Constitutional Role
title: EU Law | M2 L4: The Court of Justice - Structure, Jurisdiction and Constitutional Role
description: The Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU) is the supreme interpreter of EU law. This episode covers the structure of the CJEU and General Court, their respective jurisdictions, the Court's constitutional role in developing EU law through landmark judgments, and why understanding CJEU jurisprudence is central to the FE-1.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M2 L5: Democratic Deficit and Institutional Accountability
title: EU Law | M2 L5: Democratic Deficit and Institutional Accountability
description: The EU has long been criticised for its democratic deficit — the perception that EU decision-making lacks sufficient democratic legitimacy. This episode examines the arguments for and against this critique, the institutional reforms introduced by the Lisbon Treaty, and what democratic accountability looks like in a supranational organisation.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M3 L1: Primary Law - Treaties and the Charter
title: EU Law | M3 L1: Primary Law - Treaties and the Charter
description: EU primary law consists of the Treaties and the Charter of Fundamental Rights. This episode covers the TEU and TFEU, the hierarchy of EU norms, the legal status of the Charter following Lisbon, and how primary law constrains all other EU action. The constitutional bedrock of the entire subject.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M3 L2: Secondary Law - Regulations, Directives and Decisions
title: EU Law | M3 L2: Secondary Law - Regulations, Directives and Decisions
description: Most EU law takes effect through secondary legislation under Article 288 TFEU. This episode distinguishes regulations (directly applicable), directives (requiring national implementation), and decisions (binding on addressees), and explains why the distinction matters enormously for questions of direct effect and Member State liability.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M3 L3: Delegated Acts, Implementing Acts and Comitology
title: EU Law | M3 L3: Delegated Acts, Implementing Acts and Comitology
description: The Commission can be empowered to adopt delegated and implementing acts under Articles 290 and 291 TFEU. This episode covers the distinction between these instruments, the comitology system for supervising Commission implementing powers, and the constitutional significance of the Lisbon Treaty's reform of this area.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M4 L1: General Principles of EU Law
title: EU Law | M4 L1: General Principles of EU Law
description: The CJEU has developed a body of general principles — including proportionality, legal certainty, legitimate expectations, and fundamental rights — that bind EU institutions and Member States when implementing EU law. This episode covers the main principles, their origins, and their application in FE-1 relevant case law.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M4 L2: Fundamental Rights - Development and the Charter
title: EU Law | M4 L2: Fundamental Rights - Development and the Charter
description: Fundamental rights protection began in the EU as judge-made law before being codified in the Charter. This episode traces that development — from Stauder and Internationale Handelsgesellschaft through to the Lisbon Charter — and examines the scope of the Charter as a binding legal instrument.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M4 L3: Scope of the Charter - When Does It Apply to Member States?
title: EU Law | M4 L3: Scope of the Charter - When Does It Apply to Member States?
