description: The Charter binds Member States only when they are implementing EU law — but when exactly does that occur? This episode examines Article 51 of the Charter, the CJEU's interpretation of its scope in cases like Fransson and Ispas, and the ongoing controversy about how broadly or narrowly the Charter's application to national measures should be read.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M5 L1: Supremacy of EU Law - Costa, Simmenthal and the Constitutional Dimension
title: EU Law | M5 L1: Supremacy of EU Law - Costa, Simmenthal and the Constitutional Dimension
description: The supremacy of EU law is the fundamental constitutional principle of the EU legal order. This episode covers the foundational cases — Costa v ENEL [1964] and Simmenthal [1978] — the absolute nature of the supremacy claim, and how national courts (including Irish courts) have responded to it.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M5 L2: Direct Effect of Treaty Articles and Regulations - Van Gend and Defrenne
title: EU Law | M5 L2: Direct Effect of Treaty Articles and Regulations - Van Gend and Defrenne
description: Direct effect means that EU law provisions can create individual rights enforceable in national courts. This episode covers the criteria established in Van Gend en Loos [1963], the application to treaty articles and regulations, the distinction between vertical and horizontal direct effect, and the landmark Defrenne cases.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M5 L3: Direct Effect of Directives - Marshall, Van Duyn, Ratti and Emanation of the State
title: EU Law | M5 L3: Direct Effect of Directives - Marshall, Van Duyn, Ratti and Emanation of the State
description: Directives can have direct effect once the implementation deadline has passed. This episode covers the conditions for directive direct effect, the vertical/horizontal distinction, the emanation of the State doctrine from Marshall and Foster, and why the FE-1 regularly tests candidate ability to identify whether direct effect is available.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M5 L4: Indirect Effect - Von Colson, Marleasing and Limits in Criminal Cases
title: EU Law | M5 L4: Indirect Effect - Von Colson, Marleasing and Limits in Criminal Cases
description: Where direct effect is unavailable, indirect effect may provide a remedy. The duty of consistent interpretation — from Von Colson and Marleasing — requires national courts to interpret domestic law in light of EU directives. This episode covers the principle, its limits, and the prohibition on indirect effect being used against individuals in criminal proceedings.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M6 L1: The Francovich Principle - State Liability for Failure to Implement EU Law
title: EU Law | M6 L1: The Francovich Principle - State Liability for Failure to Implement EU Law
description: If a Member State fails to implement a directive and an individual suffers loss as a result, the State may be liable in damages. This episode examines Francovich [1991], the three conditions for State liability, and how this doctrine effectively extended EU law enforcement to situations where direct effect was unavailable.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M6 L2: Brasserie du Pecheur, Factortame and the Sufficiently Serious Breach
title: EU Law | M6 L2: Brasserie du Pecheur, Factortame and the Sufficiently Serious Breach
description: The Francovich principle was extended in Brasserie du Pêcheur and Factortame III to cover all forms of Member State breach of EU law. This episode examines the sufficiently serious breach test, the factors courts consider, and the cases that have shaped how Francovich damages are assessed in practice.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M6 L3: National Procedural Autonomy and its Limits - Equivalence and Effectiveness
title: EU Law | M6 L3: National Procedural Autonomy and its Limits - Equivalence and Effectiveness
description: EU law claims but does not always impose procedural rules. The principle of national procedural autonomy allows Member States to set their own rules for enforcement — subject to the equivalence and effectiveness requirements. This episode examines what those requirements demand and the cases in which national procedural rules have been found to breach EU law.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M7 L1: Judicial Review of EU Acts - Article 263 TFEU
title: EU Law | M7 L1: Judicial Review of EU Acts - Article 263 TFEU
description: Article 263 TFEU allows the CJEU to review the legality of EU acts. This episode covers the reviewable acts, the four grounds of review — lack of competence, infringement of an essential procedural requirement, infringement of the Treaties, and misuse of powers — and the time limit for bringing an action.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M7 L2: Standing Under Article 263 - Privileged, Quasi-Privileged and Non-Privileged Applicants
title: EU Law | M7 L2: Standing Under Article 263 - Privileged, Quasi-Privileged and Non-Privileged Applicants
description: Not everyone can bring an annulment action. This episode examines the three categories of applicant under Article 263 TFEU, the restrictive standing requirements for private individuals following Plaumann, the reforms of the Lisbon Treaty, and whether the current standing rules adequately protect individuals from unlawful EU action.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M7 L3: The Plea of Illegality - Article 277 TFEU
title: EU Law | M7 L3: The Plea of Illegality - Article 277 TFEU
description: Where a regulation cannot be challenged directly under Article 263, a party may nonetheless challenge its legality indirectly under Article 277 TFEU. This episode covers when the plea of illegality is available, its relationship with Article 263, and the scope of its application to different categories of EU act.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M7 L4: Article 265 TFEU - Action for Failure to Act
title: EU Law | M7 L4: Article 265 TFEU - Action for Failure to Act
description: Article 265 TFEU provides a remedy where an EU institution unlawfully fails to act. This episode covers the conditions for bringing an action — including the requirement to first call on the institution to act — the standing rules, and the relationship between Article 265 and Article 263 actions.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M8 L1: The Preliminary Reference Mechanism - Article 267 TFEU and Foto-Frost
title: EU Law | M8 L1: The Preliminary Reference Mechanism - Article 267 TFEU and Foto-Frost
description: Article 267 TFEU allows (and sometimes requires) national courts to refer questions of EU law to the CJEU. This episode covers the mechanism, the Foto-Frost principle (national courts cannot declare EU acts invalid), and why the preliminary reference procedure is the main way EU law reaches national courts and FE-1 exams.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M8 L2: The Mandatory Duty to Refer and Acte Clair - CILFIT
title: EU Law | M8 L2: The Mandatory Duty to Refer and Acte Clair - CILFIT
description: Courts of last resort must refer questions of EU law unless the answer is already clear. This episode examines the CILFIT criteria for acte clair, the conditions under which a court can decline to refer, and the debate about whether national courts apply these conditions too liberally.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M8 L3: Effect of Preliminary Rulings and Temporal Limitations
title: EU Law | M8 L3: Effect of Preliminary Rulings and Temporal Limitations
description: A ruling of the CJEU on EU law has retroactive effect — it declares what the law has always been. This episode covers the general and retroactive effect of preliminary rulings, the exceptional power of the Court to limit the temporal effects of a ruling, and the Irish cases that have raised questions about preliminary references.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M9 L1: The Customs Union - Articles 28 to 30 TFEU
title: EU Law | M9 L1: The Customs Union - Articles 28 to 30 TFEU
description: The Customs Union lies at the heart of the EU single market. This episode examines the prohibition on customs duties and charges having equivalent effect under Articles 28 to 30 TFEU, the meaning of charges having equivalent effect, and the limited exceptions that justify inspection fees.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M9 L2: Article 110 TFEU - Internal Taxation and Discrimination
title: EU Law | M9 L2: Article 110 TFEU - Internal Taxation and Discrimination
description: Article 110 TFEU prohibits Member States from using internal taxation to discriminate against imported goods. This episode covers the two limbs of Article 110 — direct discrimination and indirect protection — the assessment of 'similar' and 'competing' goods, and the case law including Humblot and Commission v France.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M9 L3: Quantitative Restrictions and MEQRs - Dassonville, Cassis and Keck
title: EU Law | M9 L3: Quantitative Restrictions and MEQRs - Dassonville, Cassis and Keck
description: Articles 34 and 35 TFEU prohibit quantitative restrictions and measures having equivalent effect on free movement of goods. This episode covers the Dassonville formula, the Cassis de Dijon principle of mutual recognition, the Keck departure for selling arrangements, and how courts identify a measure having equivalent effect.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: EU Law | M9 L4: Article 36 TFEU - Justifications for Restricting Free Movement of Goods
title: EU Law | M9 L4: Article 36 TFEU - Justifications for Restricting Free Movement of Goods
description: Even restrictions caught by Articles 34 and 35 may be justified. Article 36 TFEU provides an exhaustive list of grounds. This episode covers the permitted justifications — public morality, public policy, public security, health, and protection of industrial property — the proportionality requirement, and the mandatory requirements doctrine from Cassis.
subject: EU Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M10 L10.1: Testamentary Capacity and Formal Validity of Wills
title: Property Law | M10 L10.1: Testamentary Capacity and Formal Validity of Wills
description: A will must satisfy strict requirements to be valid. This episode examines the mental capacity requirements from Banks v Goodfellow, the formal requirements for execution under the Succession Act 1965 — including the signature and witness requirements — and the consequences of failure to comply with the formalities.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M10 L10.2: Revocation, Codicils and Alteration of Wills
title: Property Law | M10 L10.2: Revocation, Codicils and Alteration of Wills
description: A valid will can be revoked or altered. This episode covers the methods of revocation — a later will or codicil, a written declaration, and destruction with the intention to revoke — the effect of marriage and divorce on an existing will, and the legal status of alterations made to a will after execution.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M10 L10.3: Construction of Wills
title: Property Law | M10 L10.3: Construction of Wills
description: When the meaning of a will is unclear, courts must construe it. This episode examines the principles of testamentary construction — including the ordinary meaning rule, the context rule, and the use of extrinsic evidence — and the cases where courts have had to resolve ambiguities in how estates are distributed.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M10 L10.4: The Legal Right Share - Section 111
title: Property Law | M10 L10.4: The Legal Right Share - Section 111
description: A surviving spouse or civil partner has an absolute right to a share in the deceased's estate regardless of the will. Section 111 of the Succession Act 1965 sets out the legal right share. This episode covers when it arises, how it is calculated, and the priority it takes over other claims on the estate.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M10 L10.5: Provision for Children - Section 117
title: Property Law | M10 L10.5: Provision for Children - Section 117
description: Section 117 of the Succession Act 1965 allows a child to apply to court for provision out of a deceased parent's estate where the parent has failed in their moral duty. This episode examines the test, the factors courts consider, the limits on the remedy, and the key Irish cases on what constitutes a failure of moral duty.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M10 L10.6: Intestacy
title: Property Law | M10 L10.6: Intestacy
description: When a person dies without a valid will, their estate is distributed according to the intestacy rules in Part VI of the Succession Act 1965. This episode covers the full distribution scheme, the priority of the spouse and children, distribution to more remote relatives, and the position where no relatives can be found.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M1 L1.1: Relativity of Title
title: Property Law | M1 L1.1: Relativity of Title
description: Irish land law is based on a system of relative rather than absolute title. This episode explains what that means — that ownership of land is a question of who has the better claim — and examines how the system of relativity of title operates in practice, including its implications for adverse possession and conveyancing.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M1 L1.2: The Law of Finding
title: Property Law | M1 L1.2: The Law of Finding
description: Who owns property that has been found? This episode examines the rules on finding — when a finder has a good title against everyone except the true owner, the exception for things found embedded in land, the obligations of finders, and the cases that have wrestled with ownership of found objects in unusual circumstances.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M2 L2.1: Joint Tenancy and Tenancy in Common
title: Property Law | M2 L2.1: Joint Tenancy and Tenancy in Common
description: Two or more people can own land together, but in fundamentally different ways. This episode examines joint tenancy (with its right of survivorship) and tenancy in common (with separate shares), the four unities required for a joint tenancy, and how to determine which type of co-ownership exists in a given case — an essential skill for FE-1.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M2 L2.2: Creation of Co-Ownership Under the LCLRA 2009
title: Property Law | M2 L2.2: Creation of Co-Ownership Under the LCLRA 2009
description: The Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009 changed the rules on creating co-ownership. This episode examines how the 2009 Act modified the pre-existing law, the default position where an instrument is ambiguous, and the practical implications for conveyancers advising clients on how to hold property.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M2 L2.3: Severance of Joint Tenancy
title: Property Law | M2 L2.3: Severance of Joint Tenancy
description: A joint tenancy can be severed — converting a joint tenancy into a tenancy in common — in a number of ways. This episode examines the methods of severance (act, mutual agreement, course of dealing), the effect of severance, and the controversial question of whether unilateral notice can sever a joint tenancy under Irish law.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M2 L2.4: Rights and Obligations of Co-Owners
title: Property Law | M2 L2.4: Rights and Obligations of Co-Owners
description: Co-owners have both rights and obligations in respect of the co-owned property. This episode covers the right to occupy, the right to an account of profits, the obligation not to commit waste, the order for partition or sale under the LCLRA 2009, and the court's approach where co-owners cannot agree.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M3 L3.1: Adverse Possession - The Basic Framework
title: Property Law | M3 L3.1: Adverse Possession - The Basic Framework
description: A person who occupies another's land without permission for the limitation period can extinguish the paper owner's title. This episode introduces the framework for adverse possession in Ireland under the Statute of Limitations 1957, the rationale for the doctrine, and how it has been modified by the LCLRA 2009.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M3 L3.2: Requirements for Adverse Possession
title: Property Law | M3 L3.2: Requirements for Adverse Possession
description: Adverse possession requires actual possession that is open, continuous, exclusive, and adverse. This episode examines each requirement in detail, the animus possidendi requirement, the meaning of adverse use, and the leading Irish cases on what conduct is and is not sufficient to establish possession.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M3 L3.3: Adverse Possession - Special Cases and Advanced Issues
title: Property Law | M3 L3.3: Adverse Possession - Special Cases and Advanced Issues
description: Adverse possession throws up complex scenarios in practice. This episode covers adverse possession against a future interest, the effect of acknowledgment, the special rules for State land, adverse possession of registered land under the Registration of Title Act 1964, and the human rights dimension following JA Pye v United Kingdom.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M4 L4.1: Nature and Requirements of Easements
title: Property Law | M4 L4.1: Nature and Requirements of Easements
description: An easement is a right to use another's land in some defined way. This episode covers the definition of an easement from Re Ellenborough Park [1956], the four requirements — dominant and servient tenement, the right must accommodate the dominant tenement, the tenements must be separately owned, and the right must be capable of forming the subject matter of a grant.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M4 L4.2: Acquisition of Easements
title: Property Law | M4 L4.2: Acquisition of Easements
description: Easements can be acquired expressly, impliedly, or by prescription. This episode covers express grant and reservation, the rule in Wheeldon v Burrows, the rule under Section 6 of the Conveyancing Act 1881, implied necessity, and the various routes to acquiring an easement on the grant of part of a landowner's estate.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M4 L4.3: Prescription
title: Property Law | M4 L4.3: Prescription
description: Prescription allows an easement to be acquired through long use. This episode examines the three methods of prescription available in Ireland — common law prescription, lost modern grant, and the Prescription Act 1832 — their requirements, their differences, and how the LCLRA 2009 reformed this area.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M4 L4.4: Extinguishment of Easements and Profits a Prendre
title: Property Law | M4 L4.4: Extinguishment of Easements and Profits a Prendre
description: Easements and profits can be brought to an end in a number of ways. This episode covers extinguishment by express release, implied release (abandonment), unity of ownership, and statutory extinguishment, and distinguishes the rules for easements from those applicable to profits à prendre.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M5 L5.1: Freehold Covenants at Common Law
title: Property Law | M5 L5.1: Freehold Covenants at Common Law
description: A covenant is a contractual promise about the use of land. At common law, the benefit of a covenant can run to successors but the burden traditionally cannot. This episode examines the rule in Tulk v Moxhay, the conditions for the transmission of benefit and burden at common law, and why the common law rules are largely insufficient on their own.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M5 L5.2: Freehold Covenants in Equity and the LCLRA 2009
title: Property Law | M5 L5.2: Freehold Covenants in Equity and the LCLRA 2009
description: Equity fills the gap left by the common law by allowing the burden of restrictive covenants to run with the land under the rule in Tulk v Moxhay. This episode covers the conditions for the burden to run in equity, the conditions for the benefit to pass, the LCLRA 2009 reforms, and the discharge and modification of freehold covenants.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M6 L6.1: The Family Home Protection Act 1976
title: Property Law | M6 L6.1: The Family Home Protection Act 1976
description: Section 3 of the Family Home Protection Act 1976 provides that a spouse cannot convey the family home without the prior written consent of the other spouse. This episode examines the scope and application of this protection, what constitutes the family home, the meaning of prior written consent, and the consequences of non-compliance.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M6 L6.2: Civil Partners and Cohabitants
title: Property Law | M6 L6.2: Civil Partners and Cohabitants
description: The Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010 extended similar protections to civil partners and created a framework for property claims by qualifying cohabitants. This episode covers the shared home protection for civil partners and the redress scheme for cohabitants on relationship breakdown or death.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M6 L6.3: Family Law Acts and Property Adjustment Orders
title: Property Law | M6 L6.3: Family Law Acts and Property Adjustment Orders
description: On the breakdown of a marriage or civil partnership, courts have extensive powers to adjust property rights. This episode covers the property adjustment order jurisdiction under the Family Law Act 1995 and the Family Law (Divorce) Act 1996, the factors courts consider, and the interaction with the general law of property.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M6 L6.4: Bankruptcy and the Family Home
title: Property Law | M6 L6.4: Bankruptcy and the Family Home
description: When one co-owner is declared bankrupt, the family home may be at risk. This episode examines the rights of a trustee in bankruptcy in respect of the family home, the balancing exercise courts must perform between the interests of creditors and the needs of the family, and the relevant provisions of the Bankruptcy Act 1988.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M7 L7.1: Historical Methods of Creating Mortgages
title: Property Law | M7 L7.1: Historical Methods of Creating Mortgages
description: Mortgages have a complex legal history. This episode traces the development of mortgage law from the common law conveyance with a proviso for redemption, through the equitable mortgage, to the modern statutory charge under the LCLRA 2009. Understanding the history is key to understanding the modern law.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M7 L7.2: Mortgagee's Remedies on Default
title: Property Law | M7 L7.2: Mortgagee's Remedies on Default
description: When a mortgagor defaults, the mortgagee has a range of remedies. This episode covers the power of sale, the right to take possession, foreclosure (now effectively abolished), the appointment of a receiver, and the procedural requirements under Irish law — including the requirement for a possession order before a bank can evict.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M7 L7.3: The Equity of Redemption and Clogs on the Equity
title: Property Law | M7 L7.3: The Equity of Redemption and Clogs on the Equity
description: Equity protects mortgagors through the equity of redemption — the right to redeem the mortgage even after the legal date for redemption has passed. This episode examines the equity of redemption, the doctrine against clogs on the equity, the unconscionability cases, and what terms in a mortgage will be struck down.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M7 L7.4: Consumer Mortgage Protections
title: Property Law | M7 L7.4: Consumer Mortgage Protections
description: Irish law provides significant protections for residential mortgage borrowers in financial difficulty. This episode covers the Central Bank's Code of Conduct on Mortgage Arrears, the Abhaile scheme, the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2013 amendments, and the court's jurisdiction to adjourn possession proceedings where a mortgagor has made genuine efforts to resolve arrears.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M8 L8.1: Types of Licences
title: Property Law | M8 L8.1: Types of Licences
description: A licence is a permission to be on land that does not create a proprietary interest. This episode introduces the different categories of licence — bare licence, contractual licence, and licence coupled with an interest — and explains why the distinction between a licence and a property right matters so much in practice.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M8 L8.2: Contractual Licences
title: Property Law | M8 L8.2: Contractual Licences
description: A contractual licence is one that arises from a contract between the parties. This episode examines when a contractual licence is revocable, the injunction remedy to prevent wrongful revocation, and the controversial question of whether a contractual licence can bind third parties — an area where Irish and English law have diverged.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M8 L8.3: Proprietary Estoppel in Land Law
title: Property Law | M8 L8.3: Proprietary Estoppel in Land Law
description: Proprietary estoppel can give rise to a right in land where a person has relied on a landowner's assurance to their detriment. This episode covers the three elements — assurance, reliance, and detriment — the Irish cases from McMahon v Kerry County Council onwards, and the discretionary nature of the remedies courts award.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M8 L8.4: Rights of Residence
title: Property Law | M8 L8.4: Rights of Residence
description: A right of residence is a personal right to occupy land and is commonly created in family settlements in Ireland. This episode examines the legal nature of a right of residence, whether it can bind successors, the tax implications, and the Irish case law on the scope and enforceability of residential rights.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M9 L9.1: Nature of a Lease and Distinction from Licence
title: Property Law | M9 L9.1: Nature of a Lease and Distinction from Licence
description: The distinction between a lease and a licence is one of the most important and frequently examined questions in Irish property law. This episode examines the essential elements of a lease under the Street v Mountford [1985] test, the Irish approach, and how courts look to substance rather than label in determining which relationship exists.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M9 L9.2: Covenants in Leases
title: Property Law | M9 L9.2: Covenants in Leases
description: A lease contains obligations (covenants) on both landlord and tenant. This episode covers the implied and express covenants, the landlord's obligation to allow quiet enjoyment, the tenant's obligation to pay rent and repair, and the rules on when covenants in leases run with the land to bind successors in title.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M9 L9.3: The Residential Tenancies Act 2004
title: Property Law | M9 L9.3: The Residential Tenancies Act 2004
description: The Residential Tenancies Act 2004 (as amended) fundamentally reformed the law governing private residential tenancies in Ireland. This episode covers the security of tenure provisions, the four-year Part 4 tenancy, the grounds for termination, the rent review process, and the role of the Residential Tenancies Board in dispute resolution.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Property Law | M9 L9.4: Commercial Leases and Ground Rents
title: Property Law | M9 L9.4: Commercial Leases and Ground Rents
description: Commercial leases are governed largely by the common law of landlord and tenant, modified by specific legislation. This episode covers the Landlord and Tenant Acts, the rights of commercial tenants to new tenancies, the ground rents legislation, and the process by which ground rent tenants can acquire the freehold interest in their property.
subject: Property Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M10 L10.1: Trespass to the Person - Battery
title: Tort Law | M10 L10.1: Trespass to the Person - Battery
description: Battery is the direct and intentional application of force to another person without consent. This episode covers the elements of battery — act, directness, intention, and lack of consent — the medical context where consent is particularly significant, and the Irish case law on what constitutes sufficient force.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M10 L10.2: Trespass to the Person - Assault and False Imprisonment
title: Tort Law | M10 L10.2: Trespass to the Person - Assault and False Imprisonment
description: Assault and false imprisonment are two distinct trespass torts that protect personal autonomy. This episode examines the elements of assault — an intentional act causing reasonable apprehension of imminent battery — and false imprisonment, the requirement of total restraint, and the cases on what constitutes a sufficiently complete restriction of movement.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M10 L10.3: Trespass - Intentional Infliction of Emotional Distress
title: Tort Law | M10 L10.3: Trespass - Intentional Infliction of Emotional Distress
description: Irish tort law recognises a cause of action for the intentional or reckless infliction of emotional distress in certain circumstances. This episode examines the elements of this tort, its relationship to the intentional trespass torts, the threshold of seriousness required, and how it interacts with the remedies available under the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M11 L11.1: Trespass to Land
title: Tort Law | M11 L11.1: Trespass to Land
description: Trespass to land is direct, intentional, and unjustified interference with another's possession of land. This episode covers the elements of the tort — including the extended reach of trespass above and below the surface — the defences of licence and justification by law, and the remedies including injunction and mesne profits.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M11 L11.2: Private Nuisance
title: Tort Law | M11 L11.2: Private Nuisance
description: Private nuisance protects a landowner's right to the reasonable use and enjoyment of their land. This episode covers the elements of private nuisance, the distinction from trespass, the factors relevant to reasonableness, the rule in Sturges v Bridgman, and the Irish cases on noise, smells, and other interferences with land enjoyment.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M11 L11.3: Public Nuisance and the Rule in Rylands v Fletcher
title: Tort Law | M11 L11.3: Public Nuisance and the Rule in Rylands v Fletcher
description: Public nuisance and the rule in Rylands v Fletcher are related but distinct heads of tortious liability. This episode examines public nuisance as both a crime and a tort, the requirements for a private action for public nuisance, and the rule in Rylands v Fletcher — whether it survives as a separate tort in Ireland following the 2009 LCLRA reforms.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M12 L12.1: Strict Liability - Liability for Animals
title: Tort Law | M12 L12.1: Strict Liability - Liability for Animals
description: Irish law imposes strict liability in certain circumstances for damage caused by animals. This episode covers the Animals Act (incorporating the common law scienter action), the strict liability for dangerous animals, and the rules on cattle trespass — with attention to the modifications introduced by the Animals Act 1985 and relevant Irish case law.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M13 L13.1: Contributory Negligence and the Civil Liability Act 1961
title: Tort Law | M13 L13.1: Contributory Negligence and the Civil Liability Act 1961
description: Where the claimant contributed to their own loss, damages may be reduced. This episode covers contributory negligence under the Civil Liability Act 1961, the apportionment of fault between claimant and defendant, the standard of care applied to claimants, and the Irish cases on how courts determine respective degrees of fault.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M13 L13.2: Volenti Non Fit Injuria and Limitation of Actions
title: Tort Law | M13 L13.2: Volenti Non Fit Injuria and Limitation of Actions
description: Volenti non fit injuria — a complete defence where the claimant freely assumed the risk of harm — and the limitation period are two important aspects of tort litigation. This episode covers the requirements for volenti, how courts distinguish it from contributory negligence, and the limitation periods applicable to different tort claims under the Statute of Limitations 1957.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M14 L14.1: Remedies in Tort - Damages
title: Tort Law | M14 L14.1: Remedies in Tort - Damages
description: Damages are the primary remedy in tort law. This episode covers the heads of general and special damages, the distinction between compensatory, aggravated, and exemplary damages, the assessment of non-pecuniary loss, the multiplier/multiplicand method for future loss, and the Book of Quantum for personal injury claims in Ireland.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M14 L14.2: Remedies in Tort - Injunctions
title: Tort Law | M14 L14.2: Remedies in Tort - Injunctions
description: An injunction is an equitable remedy that can prevent the continuation of a tort. This episode covers the interlocutory injunction in the tort context, the Campus Oil test as applied in nuisance and trespass cases, mandatory injunctions, quia timet injunctions, and when Irish courts will award an injunction rather than damages.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M15 L15.1: Fatal Injuries and Survival of Actions - Civil Liability Act 1961
title: Tort Law | M15 L15.1: Fatal Injuries and Survival of Actions - Civil Liability Act 1961
description: The death of a victim raises special legal issues. This episode covers survival of actions under Part III of the Civil Liability Act 1961 (the estate's claim), the dependency claim under Part IV (the family's claim for loss of support), the assessment of fatal injury damages, and the interaction between both heads of claim.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M16 L16.1: Concurrent Wrongdoers - Civil Liability Act 1961
title: Tort Law | M16 L16.1: Concurrent Wrongdoers - Civil Liability Act 1961
description: Where two or more defendants contribute to the same loss, the rules on concurrent wrongdoing under Part III of the Civil Liability Act 1961 apply. This episode covers proportionate liability, the right of contribution between wrongdoers, and the claimant's ability to recover in full from any one concurrent wrongdoer.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M16 L16.2: Vicarious Liability - Employers and Independent Contractors
title: Tort Law | M16 L16.2: Vicarious Liability - Employers and Independent Contractors
description: An employer can be held vicariously liable for the torts of their employees committed in the course of employment. This episode covers the employee/independent contractor distinction, the course of employment test, and the modern expansion of vicarious liability to non-employment relationships following Various Claimants v Catholic Child Welfare Society [2012].
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M16 L16.3: Medical Negligence - The Dunne Principles
title: Tort Law | M16 L16.3: Medical Negligence - The Dunne Principles
description: Medical negligence is subject to special rules in Ireland derived from Dunne v National Maternity Hospital [1989]. This episode covers the Dunne principles as they apply to diagnosis, treatment, and the duty to warn patients of material risks, and examines how Irish courts have developed the law since Dunne in response to changing medical and social attitudes.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M1 L1.1: What is Tort Law?
title: Tort Law | M1 L1.1: What is Tort Law?
description: Tort law governs civil liability for wrongful acts that cause loss or harm to others. This episode introduces the conceptual foundations of tort law — its purposes, its relationship to contract and criminal law, and the general structure of liability. The starting point for understanding everything that follows in this subject.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M1 L1.2: FE-1 Exam Strategy for Tort Law
title: Tort Law | M1 L1.2: FE-1 Exam Strategy for Tort Law
description: Knowing the law is one thing — applying it effectively in an exam is another. This episode walks through the FE-1 exam approach for Tort Law, the typical question formats, how to structure a problem answer, which areas attract the most marks, and the most common mistakes candidates make in this subject.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M2 L2.1: Duty of Care - Historical Development and the Glencar Test
title: Tort Law | M2 L2.1: Duty of Care - Historical Development and the Glencar Test
description: The existence of a duty of care is the first element of negligence. This episode traces the development of duty from Donoghue v Stevenson [1932] through Anns, Caparo, and the Irish Glencar test from Glencar Explorations v Mayo County Council [2002]. Getting the duty analysis right is the foundation of every negligence answer.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M2 L2.2: Special Duty Situations - Omissions, Third Parties and Public Authorities
title: Tort Law | M2 L2.2: Special Duty Situations - Omissions, Third Parties and Public Authorities
description: The general duty of care does not extend to all situations. This episode examines the circumstances where duties are more restricted — omissions, liability for acts of third parties, and the special treatment of public authorities — with the Irish and English case law that draws the boundaries.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M2 L2.3: Pure Economic Loss and Negligent Misstatement
title: Tort Law | M2 L2.3: Pure Economic Loss and Negligent Misstatement
description: Recovery for pure economic loss is restricted in negligence. This episode covers the general exclusionary rule, the exception for negligent misstatement from Hedley Byrne [1964], the voluntary assumption of responsibility doctrine, and the Irish cases — including Bank of Ireland v Smith — that have shaped this complex area.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M3 L3.1: Breach of Duty - The Objective Standard and the Reasonable Person
title: Tort Law | M3 L3.1: Breach of Duty - The Objective Standard and the Reasonable Person
description: Breach of duty is assessed against the standard of the reasonable person. This episode examines who the reasonable person is, how courts adjust the standard for children, professionals, and those with special skills, and what the objective standard actually requires of defendants in Irish negligence cases.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M3 L3.2: Breach of Duty - Factors Courts Consider
title: Tort Law | M3 L3.2: Breach of Duty - Factors Courts Consider
description: In deciding whether a defendant breached their duty, courts weigh a number of factors. This episode covers the probability of harm, the gravity of potential injury, the cost of precautions, the social utility of the defendant's conduct, and how these factors are balanced in Irish negligence cases using the Dunne principles for professional negligence.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M4 L4.1: Factual Causation - The But-For Test
title: Tort Law | M4 L4.1: Factual Causation - The But-For Test
description: The claimant must prove the defendant's breach caused their loss. This episode examines the but-for test, its application in straightforward cases, and the difficulties it creates in cases involving multiple potential causes — including the Barker v Corus (now Sienkiewicz) material contribution exception.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M4 L4.2: Novus Actus Interveniens
title: Tort Law | M4 L4.2: Novus Actus Interveniens
description: A novus actus interveniens — a new intervening act — can break the chain of causation between the defendant's breach and the claimant's loss. This episode examines when an intervening act by a third party or the claimant themselves will break the chain, and the test of reasonable foreseeability that Irish courts apply.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M4 L4.3: Material Contribution and Complex Causation
title: Tort Law | M4 L4.3: Material Contribution and Complex Causation
description: Some cases do not fit neatly into the but-for framework. This episode covers material contribution to injury, material contribution to risk, the Fairchild exception for mesothelioma cases, and how Irish courts have approached causation in cases where scientific uncertainty makes the but-for test difficult to apply.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M5 L5.1: Remoteness - The Wagon Mound Test and the Thin Skull Rule
title: Tort Law | M5 L5.1: Remoteness - The Wagon Mound Test and the Thin Skull Rule
description: Even where causation is established, a defendant is only liable for harm that was reasonably foreseeable. This episode covers the Wagon Mound test, the type/kind distinction, the thin skull (egg-shell skull) rule, and how Irish courts have balanced these principles to determine which losses are too remote.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M6 L6.1: Psychiatric Injury - Primary Victims
title: Tort Law | M6 L6.1: Psychiatric Injury - Primary Victims
description: The law on recovery for psychiatric injury is more restrictive than for physical harm. This episode examines the primary victim category — where the claimant was within the zone of physical danger — the Page v Smith rule, its application in Irish law, and the requirement that the psychiatric harm be medically recognised.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M6 L6.2: Psychiatric Injury - Secondary Victims and Kelly v Hennessy
title: Tort Law | M6 L6.2: Psychiatric Injury - Secondary Victims and Kelly v Hennessy
description: Secondary victims suffer psychiatric injury through witnessing harm to others. This episode covers the control mechanisms from Alcock v Chief Constable and the Irish Supreme Court's adoption of a modified test in Kelly v Hennessy [1995] — proximity of relationship, proximity to the event, and perception through unaided senses.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M7 L7.1: Occupiers' Liability - The Occupiers' Liability Act 1995
title: Tort Law | M7 L7.1: Occupiers' Liability - The Occupiers' Liability Act 1995
description: The Occupiers' Liability Act 1995 replaced the common law categories of visitor, licensee, and trespasser with a statutory framework. This episode covers the three categories of entrant — visitor, recreational user, and trespasser — the duty owed to each, and the role of warning notices and exclusion clauses under the Act.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M8 L8.1: Product Liability - Common Law and the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991
title: Tort Law | M8 L8.1: Product Liability - Common Law and the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991
description: Manufacturers, suppliers, and importers can face liability when their products cause harm. This episode covers the common law position from Donoghue v Stevenson, the strict liability regime under the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991 (implementing the EU Product Liability Directive), and the defences available to producers.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M9 L9.1: Defamation - The Defamation Act 2009 and the Elements of the Tort
title: Tort Law | M9 L9.1: Defamation - The Defamation Act 2009 and the Elements of the Tort
description: The Defamation Act 2009 overhauled Irish defamation law. This episode covers the elements of the tort — a defamatory statement about the plaintiff, publication to a third party, and identification — the abolition of the distinction between libel and slander, and the single publication rule introduced by the Defamation (Amendment) Act 2024.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M9 L9.2: Defamation - Defences Under the 2009 Act
title: Tort Law | M9 L9.2: Defamation - Defences Under the 2009 Act
description: The 2009 Act provides a range of defences to a defamation claim. This episode covers truth, absolute and qualified privilege, honest opinion, innocent publication, and the fair and reasonable publication defence. Knowing which defence applies in a given scenario is a core FE-1 exam skill.
subject: Tort Law

spotifyTitle: Tort Law | M9 L9.3: Defamation - Remedies and Practical Exam Application
title: Tort Law | M9 L9.3: Defamation - Remedies and Practical Exam Application
description: Remedies in defamation include damages, correction orders, and declaratory orders under the 2009 Act. This episode covers the range of remedies, the factors courts consider in assessing damages, the prohibition on juries and the shift to judge-alone trials under the 2024 Act, and how to structure a defamation answer in the FE-1 exam.
subject: Tort Law
